spec_match.rationale: The monitor now matches ChromeStatus: it models the 255/256/257/512/1024 global TCP pool experiment, normal versus WebSocket global limits, unchanged per-host/per-proxy limits, no JS-exposed pool knob, and Resource Timing as the web-observable proxy.

interactive.rationale: Controls cover pool cap, pool family, shared versus partitioned model, attacker-held sockets, victim request profile, request lifetime, attack runs, reset, and a real same-origin Resource Timing fetch probe.

blurb_alignment.rationale: The page no longer claims memory-based resizing and instead frames the demo around the ChromeStatus experiment and its side-channel motivation.

edge_cases.rationale: Visible branches cover 255/256/257 boundaries, larger 512/1024 caps, unsaturated attacker pools, partitioned isolation, WebSocket pool family selection, and Resource Timing fallback if no entry is produced.

references.rationale: The concept links the correct ChromeStatus feature 5182293874049024 and the XS-Leaks connection-pool timing attack reference.

design_system.rationale: Inline styles, raw canvas hex colors, and the wrong ChromeStatus ID were removed; page-local button backgrounds set paired text colors and canvas colors are read from shared CSS variables.

summary: The connection pool monitor now answers the previous spec, edge-case, reference, motivation, and design-system questions.
